title: Parallel & Perpendicular Lines
blurb: Transversals and the eight angles they form; which pairs are congruent and which are supplementary; proving lines parallel; and perpendicular lines.
buildsOn: Unit 1's angle pairs (vertical, linear, complementary, supplementary) and Unit 2's proof machinery (converses, properties of equality, the Vertical Angles Theorem).

lessons.title: Transversals & the Eight Angles
lessons.concreteComparison: Picture two railway rails running side by side, never meeting — **parallel lines**. Now a road cuts straight across both tracks: that crossing road is a **transversal**. Where it slices the two rails it opens up eight angles, and this lesson is about naming them.
lessons.objective: Recognize parallel lines and a transversal; name the eight angles it forms as corresponding, alternate interior, alternate exterior, or same-side interior pairs — by position alone.
concept: **Parallel lines** are two lines in a plane that never meet, no matter how far they run. We mark them with matching arrowheads and write $m \parallel n$.
A **transversal** is a line that crosses two (or more) lines at two different points, opening **eight** angles — four at each crossing.
The four angles **between** the two lines are **interior**; the four **outside** are **exterior**.

The angles pair up by **position**: **corresponding** (same corner at each crossing), **alternate interior** (between the lines, opposite sides of the transversal), **alternate exterior** (outside, opposite sides), and **same-side interior** (between the lines, same side). These names are about location — no measuring yet.
lessons.keyIdea: A transversal cutting two lines makes 8 angles. Name each pair by position: corresponding, alternate interior/exterior, same-side interior.

facts.title: Interior angles
facts.explanation: The four angles between the two lines.

workedExamples.statement: A transversal cuts two lines. If the top crossing has angles $1,2,3,4$ and the bottom has $5,6,7,8$ (same layout), which angle corresponds to $\angle 2$?
workedExamples.solution: Corresponding angles sit in the **same corner** at each crossing. $\angle 2$ is top-right up top, so its match is the top-right below: $\angle 6$.

workedExamples.statement: Are $\angle 3$ and $\angle 6$ interior or exterior, and what pair type?
workedExamples.solution: Both lie **between** the lines (interior) and on **opposite sides** of the transversal — that's an **alternate interior** pair.

commonMistakes.text: Thinking any two lines with a transversal must be parallel.
commonMistakes.correction: A transversal can cross **any** two lines. Parallel is a separate condition — the special angle relationships come later, and only when the lines really are parallel.
**Interior** = the crowded space **between** the two lines; **exterior** = outside them.

tryIt.statement: Which angle is the corresponding partner of $\angle 3$ (bottom-left of the top crossing)?
tryIt.solution: Same corner below: bottom-left of the lower crossing is $\angle 7$.

tryIt.statement: Name the pair type of $\angle 1$ and $\angle 8$ (both outside the lines, opposite sides of the transversal).
tryIt.solution: Outside = exterior; opposite sides = alternate. **Alternate exterior.**

steps.title: Two lines and a cutter
beats: **Parallel lines** never meet — think railway rails, marked $m \parallel n$.
A **transversal** is a line crossing two others at two points.
It opens **eight** angles: four at each crossing.
First job: learn their names — pure position, no measuring.

steps.title: Meet the eight angles
steps.teach: Here are the eight angles, numbered by corner: $1,2,3,4$ up top and $5,6,7,8$ below. Tap the pair buttons to light up each type and see where it lives. (Ignore the measures for now — this lesson is about position.)

steps.title: Interior and exterior
beats: The four angles **between** the two lines are **interior**: $\angle 3, \angle 4, \angle 5, \angle 6$.
The four **outside** are **exterior**: $\angle 1, \angle 2, \angle 7, \angle 8$.
This split matters for naming the pairs.
“Interior” = the sandwich filling between the lines.

steps.title: Corresponding angles
beats: **Corresponding** angles sit in the **same corner** at each crossing.
Top-left up top ($\angle 1$) matches top-left below ($\angle 5$).
The four corresponding pairs: $(1,5), (2,6), (3,7), (4,8)$.
Think of sliding one crossing straight down onto the other.

steps.title: Name the corresponding partner
steps.intro: Same corner, other crossing.

steps.title: Corresponding or not
steps.intro: Match the corner.

problems.prompt: The corresponding partner of $\angle 1$ is —
problems.explanation: Same top-left corner at the lower crossing.

problems.prompt: $\angle 3$ and $\angle 7$ are —
Which angle is INTERIOR (between the lines)?
problems.explanation: $\angle 4$ lies between the two lines; $1$ and $8$ are outside.

steps.title: Alternate and same-side
beats: **Alternate interior**: between the lines, **opposite** sides of the transversal — $(3,6)$ and $(4,5)$.
**Alternate exterior**: outside the lines, opposite sides — $(1,8)$ and $(2,7)$.
**Same-side interior**: between the lines, the **same** side — $(3,5)$ and $(4,6)$.
“Alternate” = opposite sides; “same-side” = same side. Read the position, not the size.

steps.title: Find each pair
steps.teach: Cycle through the four pair types and watch which corners light up. Notice: alternate pairs sit on opposite sides of the slanted transversal; same-side pairs share a side.

steps.title: Name the pair type
steps.intro: Interior/exterior, then alternate/same-side.

problems.prompt: $\angle 4$ and $\angle 5$ (between the lines, opposite sides) are —
$\angle 2$ and $\angle 7$ (outside, opposite sides) are —
$\angle 3$ and $\angle 5$ (between the lines, same side) are —

steps.title: Why railroads care
steps.body: Real train tracks stay a fixed distance apart — parallel — for the whole route, and every road or bridge that crosses is a transversal. Surveyors set the crossing angle deliberately: a shallow crossing angle stretches the intersection out and is smoother and safer for both the train and the traffic above.

steps.title: Name it
steps.prompt: $\angle 1$ and $\angle 5$ sit in the same corner at each crossing. They are —

steps.title: What you learned
points: **Parallel lines** ($m \parallel n$) never meet; a **transversal** crosses two lines, making **eight** angles.
**Interior** angles ($3,4,5,6$) sit between the lines; **exterior** ($1,2,7,8$) outside.
Pairs by position: **corresponding** (same corner), **alternate interior/exterior** (opposite sides), **same-side interior** (same side).

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Let's practice naming — partners and pair types, all by position.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

problems.prompt: The corresponding partner of $\angle 4$ is —
Which set lists the INTERIOR angles?
$\angle 4$ and $\angle 5$ are —
$\angle 1$ and $\angle 8$ are —
problems.explanation: Both exterior, opposite sides of the transversal.

problems.prompt: $\angle 4$ and $\angle 6$ are —
problems.explanation: Both interior, on the same side of the transversal.

problems.prompt: A line crossing two others at two points is called a —
problems.explanation: That's the definition of a transversal.

steps.title: You've finished "Transversals & the Eight Angles"
steps.body: You can name all eight angles and their pairs by position. Next: what happens to those pairs when the two lines are PARALLEL — the payoff of this whole unit.

lessons.title: Angles & Parallel Lines
lessons.concreteComparison: Lean a ladder against a wall, then slide it to a second rung the same wall: the angle it makes doesn't change, because the wall's edges are parallel. When a transversal cuts **parallel** lines, matching angles come out equal — and this lesson pins down exactly which are equal and which instead add to $180^\circ$.
lessons.objective: Apply the parallel-line angle relationships: corresponding, alternate interior, and alternate exterior angles are congruent; same-side interior angles are supplementary.
concept: **Corresponding Angles Postulate**: if two parallel lines are cut by a transversal, each pair of **corresponding** angles is **congruent** (equal measures).

From it follow three theorems (all needing parallel lines): **alternate interior** angles are congruent, **alternate exterior** angles are congruent, and **same-side interior** angles are **supplementary** (sum $180^\circ$).
The one to burn in: three of the four pairs are **equal**, but **same-side interior** angles are **supplementary** — they add to $180^\circ$, they are NOT equal (except the special right-angle case).
None of this holds if the lines aren't parallel. Parallel is the price of admission.
lessons.keyIdea: Parallel lines cut by a transversal: corresponding, alternate interior, alternate exterior → CONGRUENT. Same-side interior → SUPPLEMENTARY (180°).

facts.title: Congruent trio
facts.explanation: Three pair types are equal — when parallel.

workedExamples.statement: Parallel lines are cut by a transversal; one angle is $118^\circ$. Its corresponding angle measures —
workedExamples.solution: Corresponding angles are congruent, so also $118^\circ$.

workedExamples.statement: Same parallel lines: an interior angle is $118^\circ$. Its same-side interior partner measures —

commonMistakes.correction: They are **supplementary** — they sum to $180^\circ$. Only corresponding, alternate interior, and alternate exterior pairs are equal.

commonMistakes.text: Using these relationships when the lines aren't parallel.
commonMistakes.correction: Every one of them **requires** $m \parallel n$. No parallel, no guarantee.

tryIt.statement: Parallel lines; an angle is $75^\circ$. Its alternate interior angle is —
Parallel lines; a same-side interior angle is $130^\circ$. Its partner is —

steps.title: The one postulate
beats: When the two lines are **parallel**, a transversal makes matching angles equal.
**Corresponding Angles Postulate**: corresponding pairs are **congruent**.
We accept it as a postulate (no proof) — then prove the rest from it.
Everything this lesson claims needs the lines to be parallel.

steps.title: Congruent — while parallel
steps.teach: The lines are parallel (note the matching marks). Corresponding angles $\angle 1$ and $\angle 5$ are equal. Try the other pair buttons: three types are equal, and one — same-side interior — instead sums to $180^\circ$.

steps.title: Three equal pairs
beats: **Corresponding** angles: congruent (the postulate).
**Alternate interior** angles: congruent (a theorem).
**Alternate exterior** angles: congruent (a theorem).
All three equal — when the lines are parallel.

steps.title: Same-side interior are supplementary
beats: **Same-side interior** angles do NOT match.
They are **supplementary**: they add to $180^\circ$.
$118^\circ$ and $62^\circ$: different, but $118 + 62 = 180$.
This is the single most-missed fact in the unit — congruent for three, $180^\circ$ for this one.

steps.title: Congruent or supplementary?
steps.intro: Match the pair type to its rule.

examples.prompt: Parallel lines; an angle is $110^\circ$. Its corresponding angle?
Parallel lines; an interior angle is $110^\circ$. Its same-side interior partner?

steps.title: Which rule?
steps.intro: Equal, or add to 180?

problems.prompt: Parallel lines; an angle is $65^\circ$. Its alternate exterior angle is —
Parallel lines; a same-side interior angle is $72^\circ$. Its partner is —
Parallel lines; an angle is $140^\circ$. Its alternate interior angle is —

steps.title: Only when parallel
steps.teach: Now tap the toggle to make the lines NOT parallel. Watch corresponding angles turn red — they're no longer equal. Parallel is exactly what makes the relationships hold.

steps.title: Two-step mixes
steps.intro: Combine with Unit 1 (vertical, linear pairs).

examples.prompt: Parallel lines; $\angle 1 = 125^\circ$. Find its same-side interior partner, then that partner's vertical angle.
steps: Vertical angle of that is also $55^\circ$.

steps.title: Name the relationship's result
steps.intro: Congruent trio vs the supplementary one.

problems.prompt: Which pair type is SUPPLEMENTARY (not congruent) on parallel lines?
Parallel lines; an angle is $90^\circ$. Its same-side interior partner is —
problems.explanation: $180 - 90 = 90$ — the one case where same-side interior angles are also equal.

steps.title: The illusion that isn't
steps.body: Op-artists draw parallel lines crossed by many short transversals at clashing angles — the famous “café wall” illusion makes the parallel lines look slanted and wonky. They're provably parallel; your visual system is just fooled by the corresponding angles. Geometry wins over the eye.

steps.title: Congruent or 180?
steps.prompt: On parallel lines, same-side interior angles are —

steps.title: What you learned
points: **Corresponding Angles Postulate** → corresponding angles congruent when parallel.
**Alternate interior** and **alternate exterior** angles are congruent too.
**Same-side interior** angles are **supplementary** ($180^\circ$) — the exception. All of it needs parallel lines.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Let's drill the congruent-vs-supplementary split until it's automatic.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

problems.prompt: Parallel lines; an angle is $105^\circ$. Its corresponding angle is —
Parallel lines; a same-side interior angle is $105^\circ$. Its partner is —
Parallel lines; an angle is $48^\circ$. Its alternate interior angle is —
Which relationship does NOT give equal angles on parallel lines?
problems.explanation: Same-side interior are supplementary, not equal.

problems.prompt: Parallel lines; an alternate exterior angle is $153^\circ$. Its partner is —
If a same-side interior pair are EQUAL, each must be —
problems.explanation: Equal and summing to $180^\circ$ forces $90^\circ$ each.

steps.title: You've finished "Angles & Parallel Lines"
steps.body: You know exactly which pairs are equal and which sum to $180^\circ$. Next: use those rules to find every one of the eight angles — including with algebra.

lessons.title: Finding Angle Measures
lessons.concreteComparison: A single beam of sunlight hitting a set of parallel window blinds makes the same angle on every slat. Know one angle where a transversal meets parallel lines, and you can find all eight — this lesson turns the relationships into a tool, algebra included.
lessons.objective: Find all eight angles formed by a transversal and parallel lines from one known angle, and solve for a variable using the relationships.
concept: Given **one** angle on parallel lines, every other angle is either **equal** to it (corresponding, alternate interior/exterior, or vertical) or its **supplement** (linear pair or same-side interior).
So all eight angles take just **two** values: the angle and its $180^\circ$ supplement.

When an angle is written as an **expression** in $x$, set up an equation: equal pairs give “expression = expression”; supplementary pairs give “expression + expression = $180$”. Then solve (Unit 2's algebraic proof).
Always check *which* relationship links the two angles before you write the equation — equal or sum-to-$180$.
lessons.keyIdea: One angle fixes all eight: each is the angle or its supplement. For algebra, set equal pairs equal and supplementary pairs to 180, then solve.

facts.title: Just two values
facts.explanation: Every one of the eight angles is one of these.

workedExamples.statement: Parallel lines cut by a transversal; $\angle 1 = 112^\circ$. Find $\angle 4$ (its vertical angle) and $\angle 2$ (its linear pair).
Corresponding angles are $(3x)^\circ$ and $(2x + 40)^\circ$. Solve for $x$.

commonMistakes.text: Setting supplementary angles equal (or vice versa).
commonMistakes.correction: Check the relationship first: equal pairs → “= ”; same-side interior / linear pairs → “sum $= 180$.”

commonMistakes.text: Stopping after finding $x$ when asked for the angle.
commonMistakes.correction: If the question wants the **angle measure**, substitute $x$ back into the expression.

tryIt.statement: Parallel lines; $\angle 5 = 47^\circ$. Its same-side interior partner $\angle 3$ is —
Alternate interior angles are $(x + 25)^\circ$ and $(2x - 10)^\circ$. Solve for $x$.

steps.title: One angle, all eight
beats: On parallel lines, every angle equals the given one or its supplement.
Equal to it: vertical, corresponding, alternate interior, alternate exterior.
Supplement of it: linear pair, same-side interior.
So the eight angles show only **two** numbers, $a$ and $180^\circ - a$.

steps.title: Read every measure
steps.teach: This figure shows the actual degrees in each corner. There are only two values — the acute and the obtuse — and they add to $180^\circ$. Tilt the transversal and watch both update.

steps.title: Fill in from one angle
beats: Say $\angle 1 = 112^\circ$.
Its supplement is $180^\circ - 112^\circ = 68^\circ$.
Every angle is now $112^\circ$ or $68^\circ$.
Match each by its relationship to $\angle 1$.

steps.title: Find the measures
steps.intro: Equal, or supplement.

examples.prompt: $\angle 1 = 112^\circ$ on parallel lines. Find $\angle 5$ (corresponding) and $\angle 3$ (same-side interior).
$\angle 6 = 73^\circ$. Find its alternate interior angle and its linear-pair neighbor.

steps.title: Fill in the angle
steps.intro: Pick equal or supplement.

problems.prompt: $\angle 2 = 121^\circ$; find its corresponding angle $\angle 6$.
$\angle 4 = 88^\circ$; find its same-side interior partner $\angle 6$.
$\angle 7 = 64^\circ$; find its alternate exterior angle $\angle 2$.

steps.title: Set up the equation
beats: Two angles given as expressions? Use the relationship.
**Equal** pairs (corresponding, alternate…): set the expressions **equal**.
**Supplementary** pairs (same-side interior, linear): set their **sum to $180$**.
Solve the equation — it's the algebraic proof from Unit 2.

steps.intro: Relationship first, then equation.

steps.intro: Equal pairs = ; supplementary pairs sum to 180.

steps.title: Blinds, bridges, and parking lots
steps.body: Angled parking spaces are drawn as parallel lines cut by the transversal of the driving lane — traffic engineers pick the angle so the equal corresponding angles let cars turn in smoothly. Too steep and you can't swing in; the geometry of this lesson is literally painted on the asphalt.

steps.title: What you learned
points: One angle fixes all eight — each is that angle or its $180^\circ$ supplement.
Equal pairs: corresponding, alternate interior/exterior, vertical.
Supplementary pairs: linear pair, same-side interior. For algebra: equal → set equal; supplementary → sum to $180$; then solve.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Let's find measures and solve for x — the everyday work of parallel-line problems.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

problems.prompt: $\angle 1 = 133^\circ$ on parallel lines. Its linear-pair neighbor $\angle 2$ is —
$\angle 3 = 78^\circ$. Its corresponding angle $\angle 7$ is —
Corresponding angles $(6x)^\circ$ and $(4x + 30)^\circ$. $x = $ —
Same-side interior angles $(3x + 10)^\circ$ and $(2x)^\circ$. $x = $ —
Alternate exterior angles $(x + 15)^\circ$ and $(2x - 25)^\circ$. $x = $ —
On parallel lines, the eight angles take how many DIFFERENT values (in general)?
problems.explanation: Just the angle and its supplement — two values.

steps.title: You've finished "Finding Angle Measures"
steps.body: You can fill in all eight angles and solve for the unknown. Next: run the logic backwards — using angles to PROVE that two lines are parallel.

lessons.title: Proving Lines Parallel
lessons.concreteComparison: A carpenter checks that two shelf brackets are parallel by measuring the angle each makes with the wall stud — if the angles match, the shelves are parallel, no need to extend the boards to infinity to see they never meet. Angle facts run **backwards** too: the right matching angles PROVE two lines are parallel.
lessons.objective: Use the converses of the parallel-line theorems to prove two lines are parallel from an angle relationship.
concept: Lesson 2 said: *if parallel, then the angles match.* The **converses** run the other way: *if the angles match, then parallel.*
**Converse of the Corresponding Angles Postulate**: if a transversal makes a pair of **corresponding** angles **congruent**, the two lines are **parallel**.
Likewise: congruent **alternate interior** angles ⟹ parallel; congruent **alternate exterior** angles ⟹ parallel; **supplementary same-side interior** angles ⟹ parallel.
This is exactly the converse skill from Unit 2 — and here every converse happens to be **true**, which is what lets us *prove* lines parallel.
lessons.keyIdea: Angle facts reverse: congruent corresponding / alternate angles (or supplementary same-side interior) PROVE the lines are parallel.

facts.title: The reverse
facts.explanation: The converse of each Lesson 2 theorem — all true.

workedExamples.statement: A transversal makes corresponding angles of $70^\circ$ and $70^\circ$. Are the two lines parallel?
workedExamples.solution: Congruent corresponding angles ⟹ parallel (converse of the postulate). Yes.

workedExamples.statement: Same-side interior angles measure $105^\circ$ and $80^\circ$. Parallel?
workedExamples.solution: For parallel, same-side interior angles must be **supplementary**: $105 + 80 = 185 \neq 180$. Not parallel.

commonMistakes.text: Requiring same-side interior angles to be equal to prove parallel.
commonMistakes.correction: For same-side interior, the parallel test is **supplementary** (sum $180^\circ$), not equal.

commonMistakes.text: Concluding parallel from angles that aren't a valid pair.
commonMistakes.correction: The two angles must actually be corresponding / alternate / same-side interior. Any old pair proves nothing.

tryIt.statement: Alternate interior angles are both $54^\circ$. Parallel?
tryIt.solution: Congruent alternate interior angles ⟹ parallel. Yes.

tryIt.statement: Same-side interior angles are $118^\circ$ and $62^\circ$. Parallel?

steps.title: Run the logic backwards
beats: Lesson 2: *parallel ⟹ matching angles.*
Now the **converse**: *matching angles ⟹ parallel.*
It's the converse skill from Unit 2 — and each of these converses is **true**.
That's what lets us **prove** two lines are parallel.

steps.title: The converse that works
steps.teach: The statement and its converse are BOTH true here — the mark of a rule that runs both ways. Flip it to see the parallel-proving direction.

config.statementNote: The Corresponding Angles Postulate — parallel gives equal corresponding angles.
config.converseNote: Its converse is also true: equal corresponding angles force the lines to be parallel. This is the tool for PROVING parallel.

steps.title: Ways to prove parallel
beats: Congruent **corresponding** angles ⟹ parallel.
Congruent **alternate interior** angles ⟹ parallel.
Congruent **alternate exterior** angles ⟹ parallel.
**Supplementary** same-side interior angles ⟹ parallel.

steps.title: Parallel or not?
steps.intro: Match the test, then check the numbers.

steps.title: Apply the converse
steps.intro: Congruent (or supplementary, for same-side).

problems.prompt: Alternate interior angles $63^\circ$ and $63^\circ$. Parallel?
Same-side interior angles $100^\circ$ and $70^\circ$. Parallel?
Corresponding angles $88^\circ$ and $92^\circ$. Parallel?
options: No — corresponding angles must be equal

steps.title: Find the value that works
beats: Sometimes an angle holds a variable.
Set up the test as an equation.
Corresponding $(2x)^\circ$ and $(x + 30)^\circ$ parallel ⟹ equal: $2x = x + 30$.
Solve: $x = 30$. That value makes the lines parallel.

steps.title: Find x for parallel
steps.intro: Write the test as an equation.

examples.prompt: For what $x$ are the lines parallel if corresponding angles are $(2x)^\circ$ and $(x + 30)^\circ$?
For what $x$ are the lines parallel if same-side interior angles are $(2x)^\circ$ and $(x + 60)^\circ$?

steps.title: Solve for parallel
steps.intro: Equal test → =; same-side test → 180.

problems.prompt: Alternate interior $(3x)^\circ$ and $(x + 50)^\circ$ — parallel when $x = $
Same-side interior $(4x)^\circ$ and $(2x)^\circ$ — parallel when $x = $

steps.title: How a robot vacuum stays straight
steps.body: Many robot vacuums drive in parallel stripes across a room. They can't see the far wall, so they check parallelism the geometry way: a gyroscope measures the angle of each pass against a fixed heading, and equal angles guarantee parallel paths — the converse theorem, running on a chip.

steps.title: Prove parallel?
steps.prompt: A transversal makes alternate exterior angles of $120^\circ$ and $120^\circ$. The lines are —

steps.title: What you learned
points: The parallel-line theorems have **true converses**: matching angles ⟹ parallel.
Congruent corresponding / alternate interior / alternate exterior ⟹ parallel.
**Supplementary** same-side interior ⟹ parallel. Set the test as an equation to solve for a variable.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Let's decide parallel-or-not, and solve for the value that forces it.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

problems.prompt: Corresponding angles $95^\circ$ and $95^\circ$. Parallel?
Same-side interior angles $88^\circ$ and $92^\circ$. Parallel?
Alternate interior angles $60^\circ$ and $70^\circ$. Parallel?
Corresponding $(5x)^\circ$ and $(3x + 40)^\circ$ — parallel when $x = $
Same-side interior $(x + 20)^\circ$ and $(x)^\circ$ — parallel when $x = $
To prove parallel from same-side interior angles, they must be —

steps.title: You've finished "Proving Lines Parallel"
steps.body: You can now prove lines parallel, not just use parallelism. Next: perpendicular lines — the special case where the transversal stands at a perfect right angle.

lessons.title: Perpendicular Lines
lessons.concreteComparison: The corner where a wall meets the floor, the cross of a window frame, the intersection of two city streets on a grid — all perfect square corners. Two lines that cross at a **right angle** are **perpendicular**, the tidiest way two lines can meet.
lessons.objective: Define perpendicular lines, use the four right angles they create, and apply the fact that two lines perpendicular to the same line are parallel.
concept: **Perpendicular lines** meet at a **right angle** ($90^\circ$). We write $m \perp n$ and mark the corner with a small square.
Two perpendicular lines actually make **four** right angles — each is $90^\circ$, because vertical angles are equal and linear pairs are supplementary ($180^\circ - 90^\circ = 90^\circ$).
If a ray splits one of those right angles, the two pieces are **complementary** — they add to $90^\circ$ (Unit 1).
A key link to parallels: **in a plane, two lines perpendicular to the same line are parallel to each other** — their equal $90^\circ$ angles make same-side interior angles sum to $180^\circ$.
lessons.keyIdea: Perpendicular = meets at 90°, giving four right angles. Two lines perpendicular to the same line are parallel.

facts.title: Perpendicular
facts.explanation: Meet at a right angle; marked with a □.

facts.title: Four right angles
facts.explanation: One right angle forces all four.

workedExamples.statement: Line $m \perp$ line $n$. One of the four angles is $90^\circ$. What are the other three?
workedExamples.solution: All $90^\circ$: vertical angles equal it, and linear pairs give $180^\circ - 90^\circ = 90^\circ$.

workedExamples.statement: A ray splits a right angle into $(2x)^\circ$ and $(x)^\circ$. Find $x$.
workedExamples.solution: The pieces are complementary: $2x + x = 90 \Rightarrow 3x = 90 \Rightarrow x = 30$.

commonMistakes.text: Thinking perpendicular lines make only one right angle.
Calling the split pieces of a right angle supplementary.
commonMistakes.correction: Pieces of a **right** angle are **complementary** ($90^\circ$ total). Supplementary ($180^\circ$) is for a straight angle.

tryIt.statement: A ray splits a right angle so one piece is $58^\circ$. The other is —
Lines $a$ and $b$ are both perpendicular to line $c$ (in a plane). How are $a$ and $b$ related?
tryIt.solution: Parallel — two lines perpendicular to the same line are parallel.

steps.title: A perfect corner
beats: **Perpendicular** lines meet at a **right angle** ($90^\circ$).
Write it $m \perp n$; mark the corner with a small square □.
Think wall-meets-floor, or a $+$ sign.
It's the cleanest way two lines can cross.

steps.title: Open it to exactly 90°
steps.teach: Drag the ray up to $90^\circ$ and the label reads **right** — that's the perpendicular case. At exactly $90^\circ$ the two rays are perpendicular.

steps.title: Four right angles
beats: Suppose two lines cross and one angle is $90^\circ$.
Its **vertical** angle is also $90^\circ$ (Unit 1).
Each **linear-pair** neighbor is $180^\circ - 90^\circ = 90^\circ$.
So all four angles are right angles — a full $+$.

steps.title: Right angles all around
steps.intro: Vertical equal, linear pair supplements.

examples.prompt: $m \perp n$; one angle is $90^\circ$. Find a neighbor.
steps: Neighbor is a linear pair with the $90^\circ$.

examples.prompt: A ray splits a right angle into $(2x)^\circ$ and $(x)^\circ$. Find $x$.
steps: Pieces of a right angle are complementary.

steps.title: Right-angle arithmetic
steps.intro: Perpendicular → 90; split pieces → complementary.

problems.prompt: $m \perp n$. How many right angles are formed?
A ray splits a right angle; one piece is $37^\circ$. The other is —
Two angles that are pieces of a right angle are —

steps.title: Perpendicular to the same line
beats: Draw a line $c$. Drop two lines $a$ and $b$, each **perpendicular** to $c$.
$c$ is a transversal; $a$ and $b$ each make $90^\circ$ with it.
Same-side interior angles: $90^\circ + 90^\circ = 180^\circ$ — supplementary.
So $a \parallel b$: two lines perpendicular to the same line are **parallel**.

steps.title: Perpendicular meets parallel
steps.intro: Two right angles make same-side interior sum 180.

examples.prompt: Lines $a$ and $b$ are both perpendicular to line $c$. Why is $a \parallel b$?
steps: $c$ is a transversal; $a$ and $b$ each meet it at $90^\circ$.

steps.title: Perpendicular and parallel
steps.intro: Right angles link the two ideas.

problems.prompt: In a plane, if $a \perp c$ and $b \perp c$, then $a$ and $b$ are —
problems.explanation: Two lines perpendicular to the same line are parallel.

problems.prompt: If $m \perp n$, the angle between them is —

steps.title: The 3-4-5 shortcut
steps.body: Builders make perfect right angles without a protractor using a rope knotted into lengths $3$, $4$, and $5$: pulled taut into a triangle, the corner between the $3$ and $4$ sides is exactly $90^\circ$. Egyptian rope-stretchers squared the pyramids this way — you'll prove *why* it works in Unit 8, Pythagoras.

steps.title: Right angle
steps.prompt: Two lines are perpendicular. Each of the four angles they form measures —

steps.title: What you learned
points: **Perpendicular** ($m \perp n$): lines meeting at a **right angle**, making **four** $90^\circ$ angles.
A ray splitting a right angle makes **complementary** pieces (sum $90^\circ$).
In a plane, two lines **perpendicular to the same line are parallel**.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Let's work right angles, complementary splits, and the perpendicular-to-parallel link.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

problems.prompt: Perpendicular lines form how many right angles?
A ray splits a right angle; one piece is $24^\circ$. The other is —
A ray splits a right angle into $(3x)^\circ$ and $(2x)^\circ$. $x = $ —
If $a \perp c$ and $b \perp c$ in a plane, then $a$ and $b$ are —
problems.explanation: Perpendicular to the same line ⟹ parallel.

problems.prompt: The pieces a ray cuts a right angle into are —
A right angle is split so one piece is $x^\circ$ and the other is $(x + 20)^\circ$. $x = $ —

steps.title: You've finished "Perpendicular Lines"
steps.body: Right angles, complementary splits, and the tie to parallels — all yours. Last in the unit: two-column proofs that put every Unit 3 fact to work.

lessons.title: Proofs with Parallel Lines
lessons.concreteComparison: You've got a full toolkit now: the parallel-line rules, their converses, the perpendicular facts, and Unit 2's proof machinery. This lesson is the workshop — two-column proofs where those tools chain together, including proving one of the parallel-line theorems from scratch.
lessons.objective: Write two-column proofs that use the parallel-line relationships, and prove the Alternate Interior Angles Theorem from the Corresponding Angles Postulate.
concept: Every Unit 3 fact is a licensed **reason** in a proof: the Corresponding Angles Postulate, the alternate-angle theorems, the same-side-interior rule, and their converses.

The **Alternate Interior Angles Theorem** (Lesson 2, used but not proven) can now be proven: corresponding angles are congruent (postulate), vertical angles are congruent (Unit 2), and the Transitive Property links them.
As in Unit 2, each statement earns a reason drawn only from **earlier** facts — never the theorem being proven.
The pattern repeats everywhere ahead: state what's given, chain licensed steps, reach the goal.
lessons.keyIdea: Chain Unit 3's angle rules (and their converses) as reasons. The alternate interior theorem follows from corresponding + vertical + Transitive.

workedExamples.statement: Parallel lines cut by a transversal; $\angle 1 = 118^\circ$ and $\angle 1, \angle 5$ are corresponding. Prove $\angle 5 = 118^\circ$.
workedExamples.solution: $\angle 1 \cong \angle 5$ (Corresponding Angles Postulate), so $m\angle 5 = m\angle 1 = 118^\circ$ (definition of congruent angles / substitution).

workedExamples.statement: $\angle 3$ and $\angle 5$ are same-side interior on parallel lines, with $m\angle 3 = (2x)^\circ$ and $m\angle 5 = (x + 30)^\circ$. Prove $x = 50$.

commonMistakes.text: Using a theorem to prove itself.
commonMistakes.correction: To prove the Alternate Interior Angles Theorem you may cite corresponding and vertical angles — but **not** the alternate interior theorem itself.

commonMistakes.text: Skipping the reason on an angle step.
commonMistakes.correction: “$\angle 1 \cong \angle 5$” needs its license: *Corresponding Angles Postulate*. No naked statements.

tryIt.statement: Alternate exterior angles on parallel lines are $(4x)^\circ$ and $(2x + 50)^\circ$. Prove $x = 25$.
In proving the Alternate Interior Angles Theorem, which property links the corresponding angle and the vertical angle?
tryIt.solution: The Transitive Property — both are congruent to the same middle angle $\angle 1$.

steps.title: The full toolkit
beats: Every Unit 3 rule is now a legal **reason**.
Postulate, the alternate/same-side theorems, and all the converses.
Plus Unit 2's properties of equality and the Vertical Angles Theorem.
Time to chain them into proofs.

steps.title: Prove ∠5 = 118°
steps.teach: A short chain: the postulate gives congruence, then congruence gives equal measures.

config.given: $m \parallel n$ cut by a transversal; $\angle 1$ and $\angle 5$ are corresponding; $m\angle 1 = 118^\circ$.

steps.title: Proving alternate interior
beats: Goal: **prove** alternate interior angles $\angle 3$ and $\angle 5$ are congruent.
We may use only earlier facts: corresponding and vertical angles.
$\angle 1$ and $\angle 5$ are corresponding; $\angle 1$ and $\angle 3$ are vertical.
Both routes reach $\angle 5$ and $\angle 3$ through $\angle 1$ — Transitive is loaded.

steps.title: The Alternate Interior Angles Theorem
steps.teach: You USED this in Lesson 2. Now you PROVE it — from the corresponding-angles postulate and vertical angles, exactly the way Unit 2 proved vertical angles.

config.given: $m \parallel n$ cut by a transversal; $\angle 3$ and $\angle 5$ are alternate interior angles.

steps.title: Prove with the rules
steps.intro: Name the parallel-line reason.

examples.prompt: Alternate interior angles on parallel lines: $(3x)^\circ$ and $(x + 40)^\circ$. Prove $x = 20$.
steps: Alternate interior angles are congruent — equal measures.

examples.prompt: Same-side interior angles $(2x + 10)^\circ$ and $(3x)^\circ$ on parallel lines. Prove $x = 34$.

steps.title: Supply the reason
steps.intro: Which Unit 3 fact licenses the step?

problems.prompt: In the warm-up proof, what justifies $\angle 1 \cong \angle 5$?
problems.explanation: $\angle 1$ and $\angle 5$ are corresponding on parallel lines.

problems.prompt: In the alternate-interior proof, $\angle 1 \cong \angle 3$ is justified by —
Why can't the Alternate Interior Angles Theorem be a reason inside its own proof?
options: A proof may cite only earlier facts

steps.title: Converses in proofs
beats: The converses prove **lines parallel** inside a proof.
Given congruent corresponding angles as a statement…
…the reason *Converse of the Corresponding Angles Postulate* concludes $m \parallel n$.
Same machinery, aimed at a parallel conclusion.

steps.title: Prove it parallel
steps.intro: Cite the converse.

problems.prompt: Given: alternate interior angles are congruent. The reason that concludes $m \parallel n$ is —
options: Converse of the Alternate Interior Angles Theorem
problems.explanation: The converse turns congruent alt. interior angles into parallel lines.

problems.prompt: Corresponding angles $(5x)^\circ$ and $(3x + 40)^\circ$; find $x$ that PROVES the lines parallel.

steps.title: Every proof you'll ever write
steps.body: The chain you just built — postulate, then a known theorem, then Transitive — is the skeleton of essentially every geometry proof to come. Triangle congruence, similar figures, circle theorems: all of them are longer versions of this same “license each step from what's already proven” move.

steps.title: Name the reason
steps.prompt: On parallel lines, $\angle 1 \cong \angle 5$ (same corner). The reason is the —
steps.explanation: Same-corner pair on parallel lines = Corresponding Angles Postulate.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Every Unit 3 rule — postulate, theorems, and converses — is a legal proof **reason**.
The **Alternate Interior Angles Theorem** follows from corresponding + vertical + **Transitive**.
Converses prove lines **parallel** inside a proof. Each step cites only earlier facts.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: The unit's last practice: name reasons, prove a theorem's step, and solve within a proof.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

problems.prompt: $\angle 1 \cong \angle 5$ on parallel lines (same corner). Reason?
Proving the alternate interior theorem, $\angle 3 \cong \angle 5$ follows from $\angle 1 \cong \angle 5$ and $\angle 1 \cong \angle 3$ by the —
problems.explanation: Both congruent to $\angle 1$ ⟹ congruent to each other.

problems.prompt: Alternate interior angles $(4x)^\circ$ and $(x + 60)^\circ$ on parallel lines. $x = $ —
Same-side interior angles $(x)^\circ$ and $(4x)^\circ$ on parallel lines. $x = $ —
Given congruent corresponding angles, the reason concluding $m \parallel n$ is the —
options: Converse of the Corresponding Angles Postulate

problems.prompt: A statement in a two-column proof with no reason beside it is —
problems.explanation: Every statement needs a reason — the whole discipline of proof.

steps.title: You've finished "Proofs with Parallel Lines"
steps.body: That completes Unit 3 — you can find angles, prove lines parallel, and chain the rules into real proofs. Unit 4 turns to triangles, where these angle facts explain why every triangle's angles sum to exactly $180^\circ$.

practice.statement: Lines $\ell \parallel m$ are cut by a transversal. Corresponding angles measure $(3x + 20)°$ and $74°$. Find $x$.
practice.solution: Corresponding angles on parallel lines are equal: $3x + 20 = 74 \Rightarrow 3x = 54 \Rightarrow x = 18$.

practice.statement: Alternate interior angles measure $(5x - 10)°$ and $(3x + 22)°$ with the lines parallel. Find $x$ and the angle.
practice.solution: Alternate interior angles are equal: $5x - 10 = 3x + 22 \Rightarrow 2x = 32 \Rightarrow x = 16$. The angle is $5(16) - 10 = 70°$.

practice.statement: Same-side interior angles measure $(2x + 30)°$ and $(3x + 10)°$ with the lines parallel. Find both angles.
practice.solution: Same-side interior angles are supplementary: $(2x+30) + (3x+10) = 180 \Rightarrow 5x + 40 = 180 \Rightarrow x = 28$. The angles are $86°$ and $94°$.

practice.statement: A transversal crosses parallel lines and one angle measures $118°$. Find the measures of its vertical angle, its corresponding angle, and its same-side interior partner.
practice.solution: Vertical: $118°$ (equal). Corresponding: $118°$ (equal, parallel lines). Same-side interior partner: $180 - 118 = 62°$ (supplementary). Every one of the eight angles is $118°$ or $62°$.

practice.statement: A transversal makes alternate interior angles of $63°$ and $63°$ with lines $a$ and $b$. What can you conclude, and by which theorem?
practice.solution: Equal alternate interior angles force $a \parallel b$ — the Converse of the Alternate Interior Angles Theorem. ($63 = 63$ is exactly the hypothesis it needs.)

practice.statement: Lines $m \perp n$ meet where one of the four angles is marked $(4x + 10)°$. Find $x$.
With parallel lines, alternate exterior angles measure $(7x - 5)°$ and $(5x + 31)°$. Find the angle measure.
practice.solution: Alternate exterior angles are equal: $7x - 5 = 5x + 31 \Rightarrow 2x = 36 \Rightarrow x = 18$. The angle is $7(18) - 5 = 121°$.

practice.statement: A transversal crosses parallel lines; one interior angle is $57°$. Find its alternate interior partner and its same-side interior partner.
practice.solution: Alternate interior partner: $57°$ (equal). Same-side interior partner: $180 - 57 = 123°$ (supplementary).

testYourself.statement: Corresponding angles measure $(2x + 15)°$ and $(4x - 45)°$ on parallel lines. Find the angle.
Same-side exterior angles measure $(x + 40)°$ and $(3x)°$ on parallel lines. Find both.
testYourself.solution: Same-side exterior angles are supplementary: $x + 40 + 3x = 180 \Rightarrow 4x = 140 \Rightarrow x = 35$. The angles are $75°$ and $105°$.

testYourself.statement: For what value of $x$ do angles of $(6x - 14)°$ and $(4x + 20)°$ in alternate exterior position force the lines to be parallel?
testYourself.solution: They must be equal: $6x - 14 = 4x + 20 \Rightarrow 2x = 34 \Rightarrow x = 17$; each is $88°$.

testYourself.statement: If $t \perp \ell$ and $\ell \parallel m$, what angle does $t$ make with $m$? Why?
testYourself.solution: $90°$. A line perpendicular to one of two parallel lines is perpendicular to the other — corresponding angles carry the right angle across: the $90°$ at $\ell$ corresponds to the angle at $m$.

testYourself.statement: Three parallel lines are cut by one transversal. The angle at the top line is $71°$. What are the corresponding angles at the middle and bottom lines?
testYourself.solution: Corresponding angles are equal at every parallel line the transversal crosses: both are $71°$.

testYourself.statement: Two straight rails are crossed by a straight footpath. One crossing angle measures $104°$ and the alternate interior angle at the other rail measures $104°$. Are the rails parallel?
testYourself.solution: Yes. Equal alternate interior angles ($104° = 104°$) are exactly the converse condition for parallel lines.
